quotes.verbatim_quote:
The first picking exercise separates the bass from the melody.

This type of comping pattern is helpful when there’s no bassist in your group,
when you play solo guitar, or for technical development.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the core purpose and use cases for the bass-first picking pattern, establishing when and why a guitarist would choose this approach.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Start by learning the bass first pattern, then melody first pattern, then blend them
together to take things to the next level in your comping.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes the explicit pedagogical sequence for building comping technique, treating pattern integration as a progressive skill.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Often times you learn chords and then strum and pluck them without altering the
picking.

This is leaving a lot of room to explore in your comping by expanding on your
picking hand approach to these chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the core methodological principle of the chapter — that the picking hand, not just chord shapes, is the key variable in advanced comping.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Bringing out the melody is useful for comping, chord soloing, and especially chord
melody playing.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the musical contexts where the melody-first picking pattern applies, connecting the technique to specific jazz guitar styles.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Work this pattern as written, then play with the rhythms and take it to other keys
if you’re ready.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a two-stage practice rule — first internalise as written, then vary rhythm and transpose — applicable to both exercises in the chapter.
